meta.setting: Wet mountain pack trail on a Hoopa Valley approach
meta.rdr_clone_target: PS3-era RDR-clone western art, grounded in pack freight, animal handling, and rugged Native community work

research_grounding.title: Hoopa Valley Tribe
research_grounding.use: Hoopa Valley sovereignty, culture, traditions, natural resources, and community future.

research_grounding.title: Karuk Tribe
research_grounding.use: Northern California river and stewardship context.

image_prompt.subject: Hoopa Valley packers adjusting mule loads on a steep forest road, with family members and freight crates staged for a difficult climb
image_prompt.scene: damp mountain morning; fir and redwood shade, river mist below, one mule leaning against the grade, rawhide lashings being tightened, practical 1899 clothing, no ceremonial staging
image_prompt.composition: square diagonal climb with mules and people forming a work rhythm across the frame; load straps and hands are the visual center, not a posed face
image_prompt.camera: low trail-side angle focused on knots, load balance, mule stance, and the grade ahead
material_cues: crate wood with no readable labels
mule hair and worn saddle pads
image_prompt.hand_cut_note: Make the expertise visible in hands, knots, and spacing between animals. The trail should look steep enough to make bad packing an immediate problem.

style_modifiers.medium: PS3-era open-world western game art, early-HD in-engine trail work scene
render_traits: slightly simplified human, mule, and crate silhouettes
modest texture resolution on canvas, rawhide, bark, mud, and wool
hard ambient occlusion under pack loads, animal legs, and wet roots
cool damp forest light with river mist below
work rhythm composition instead of posed spectacle
style_modifiers.palette: wet bark brown, fir green, river mist gray, canvas tan, mule brown, rawhide cream, mud black, and cold morning blue.

text_rendering.instructions: No readable crate labels, signs, writing, or invented Native text.

negative_prompt: copy of commercial game characters, missions, towns, or logos

self_check: Reads as PS3-era western game art at square page-card scale.
The people, mules, load straps, crates, and steep trail are fully readable.
Native presence is ordinary, respectful, skilled, and grounded in work.
No invented sacred detail, fake language, costume spectacle, or modern object appears.
No copied game characters, readable modern text, or inherited non-western symbols.
